nativeRange.habitat: The Narrow-Lined Puffer inhabits muddy substrates in shallow estuaries, sheltered reef flats, lagoons, seagrass beds, and sandy areas. Juveniles have been found in mangrove habitats, and the species occurs at depths ranging from 2 to 20 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on benthic macrofauna including molluscs, crustaceans, and invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied meaty diet including squid, krill, clams, shrimp, and other meaty foods. Hard-shelled prey is generally recommended to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like fused teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: D. Terver, Muséum-Aquarium de Nancy. Non-commercial use only.

summary: The Narrow-Lined Puffer is a mid-sized pufferfish from the Western Pacific, identified by the many fine dark lines running along its body. It tends to be semi-aggressive and is generally best kept singly in a fish-only system, well away from invertebrates and corals.
careNotes:
The Narrow-Lined Puffer (Arothron manilensis) occurs across the Western Pacific from the Philippines and Borneo to Samoa and northern Australia, typically inhabiting shallow seagrass beds, sandy estuaries, sheltered reef flats, and lagoons at depths of 2–20 m. Its body is covered with small prickles, and many narrow longitudinal dark stripes run along the body — though the stripes can appear faint depending on the individual's mood or condition.

Like all members of the family Tetraodontidae, the Narrow-Lined Puffer possesses a beak-like structure of fused teeth that continues to grow throughout its life. Offering hard-shelled foods such as clams, snails, and crabs is generally advised to keep the teeth worn down and prevent overgrowth. The species lacks pelvic fins and propels itself using its pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins instead.

The Narrow-Lined Puffer has been observed tolerating slightly brackish conditions in the wild, but aquarium specimens are generally maintained at standard marine salinity (SG 1.020–1.025).

All Arothron puffers contain tetrodotoxin in their skin and organs. Under extreme stress or at death, some members of the family have been known to release toxins capable of harming tankmates; this risk is generally managed by keeping conditions stable and avoiding situations that would severely stress the fish. When moving this species, a container is generally recommended rather than a net, to prevent the fish from inflating with air — an event that can be fatal if it occurs out of water.

The Narrow-Lined Puffer is a carnivore that will readily consume invertebrates, including crustaceans and molluscs. It is not considered reef-safe and has been known to nip at corals and sessile invertebrates. It tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and similar-shaped fish, and is generally advised to be kept singly.

Given the species' maximum size of around 31 cm and its active swimming habits, a minimum aquarium volume of around 500 L with a footprint of at least 150 cm in length is generally recommended. Heavy filtration is generally advised because puffers tend to be messy eaters.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been documented in captivity. The species is oviparous in the wild. No captive breeding protocols are available in the literature.
